children.name: Casuarius
children.description: Large, flightless birds with a striking helmet-like casque on the head and brightly coloured wattled necks. They are solitary, frugivorous inhabitants of tropical rainforests and are considered keystone seed dispersers in New Guinea and northern Australia.

children.name: Casuarius casuarius
children.description: The third-tallest and second-heaviest living bird, standing up to 1.8 metres. It sports a glossy black plumage, vivid blue neck and a tall brown casque. Found in the rainforests of northern Queensland, New Guinea and nearby islands, it is famously capable of delivering powerful kicks with its dagger-like claws.

children.name: Casuarius unappendiculatus
children.description: Endemic to the lowland forests of northern and western New Guinea. It resembles the Southern Cassowary but has a brighter, more extensive orange-yellow neck and wattle. It is the largest of the cassowaries by weight and feeds primarily on fallen fruits.

children.name: Casuarius bennetti
children.description: The smallest cassowary species, standing just over one metre tall and weighing up to 25 kilograms. It inhabits the highland forests of New Guinea and nearby islands at elevations up to 3,000 metres. Its casque is flatter than that of its larger relatives, and it is more secretive and seldom observed.

children.name: Dromaius
children.description: A monotypic genus of the large, flightless emu, the second-tallest living bird after the ostrich. Highly adapted to the arid and variable conditions of the Australian continent.

children.name: Dromaius novaehollandiae
children.description: Australia's largest native bird, standing up to 1.9 metres tall with shaggy brown plumage and a long bare neck. It is a nomadic wanderer of the Outback, covering vast distances in search of food and water. Emus are curious, powerful runners capable of sprinting at 50 km/h and are the only birds with calf muscles.
